content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/vpulim/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.1.0/skills/subagent-driven-development

# Subagent-Driven Development

Execute plan by dispatching fresh subagent per task, with two-stage review after each: spec compliance review first, then code quality review.

**Why subagents:** You delegate tasks to specialized agents with isolated context. By precisely crafting their instructions and context, you ensure they stay focused and succeed at their task. They should never inherit your session's context or history ŌĆö you construct exactly what they need. This also preserves your own context for coordination work.

**Core principle:** Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review (spec then quality) = high quality, fast iteration

**Continuous execution:** Do not pause to check in with your human partner between tasks. Execute all tasks from the plan without stopping. The only reasons to stop are: BLOCKED status you cannot resolve, ambiguity that genuinely prevents progress, or all tasks complete. "Should I continue?" prompts and progress summaries waste their time ŌĆö they asked you to execute the plan, so execute it.

## When to Use

```dot
digraph when_to_use {
    "Have implementation plan?" [shape=diamond];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" [shape=diamond];
    "Stay in this session?" [shape=diamond];
    "subagent-driven-development" [shape=box];
    "executing-plans" [shape=box];
    "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [shape=box];

    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Tasks mostly independent?" [label="yes"];
    "Have implementation plan?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Stay in this session?" [label="yes"];
    "Tasks mostly independent?" -> "Manual execution or brainstorm first" [label="no - tightly coupled"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "subagent-driven-development" [label="yes"];
    "Stay in this session?" -> "executing-plans" [label="no - parallel session"];
}
```

**vs. Executing Plans (parallel session):**
- Same session (no context switch)
- Fresh subagent per task (no context pollution)
- Two-stage review after each task: spec compliance first, then code quality
- Faster iteration (no human-in-loop between tasks)

## The Process

```dot
digraph process {
    rankdir=TB;

    subgraph cluster_per_task {
        label="Per Task";
        "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent asks questions?" [shape=diamond];
        "Answer questions, provide context" [shape=box];
        "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [shape=box];
        "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [shape=box];
        "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" [shape=diamond];
        "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [shape=box];
        "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    }

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" [shape=box];
    "More tasks remain?" [shape=diamond];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [shape=box];
    "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch" [shape=box style=filled fillcolor=lightgreen];

    "Read plan, extract all tasks with full text, note context, create TodoWrite" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" -> "Implementer subagent asks questions?";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Answer questions, provide context" [label="yes"];
    "Answer questions, provide context" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)";
    "Implementer subagent asks questions?" -> "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent implements, tests, commits, self-reviews" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)";
    "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?";
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes spec gaps" -> "Dispatch spec reviewer subagent (./spec-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Spec reviewer subagent confirms code matches spec?" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" -> "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?";
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" [label="no"];
    "Implementer subagent fixes quality issues" -> "Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent (./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md)" [label="re-review"];
    "Code quality reviewer subagent approves?" -> "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" [label="yes"];
    "Mark task complete in TodoWrite" -> "More tasks remain?";
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch implementer subagent (./implementer-prompt.md)" [label="yes"];
    "More tasks remain?" -> "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" [label="no"];
    "Dispatch final code reviewer subagent for entire implementation" -> "Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch";
}
```

## Model Selection

Use the least powerful model that can handle each role to conserve cost and increase speed.

**Mechanical implementation tasks** (isolated functions, clear specs, 1-2 files): use a fast, cheap model. Most implementation tasks are mechanical when the plan is well-specified.

**Integration and judgment tasks** (multi-file coordination, pattern matching, debugging): use a standard model.

**Architecture, design, and review tasks**: use the most capable available model.

**Task complexity signals:**
- Touches 1-2 files with a complete spec ŌåÆ cheap model
- Touches multiple files with integration concerns ŌåÆ standard model
- Requires design judgment or broad codebase understanding ŌåÆ most capable model

## Handling Implementer Status

Implementer subagents report one of four statuses. Handle each appropriately:

**DONE:** Proceed to spec compliance review.

**DONE_WITH_CONCERNS:** The implementer completed the work but flagged doubts. Read the concerns before proceeding. If the concerns are about correctness or scope, address them before review. If they're observations (e.g., "this file is getting large"), note them and proceed to review.

**NEEDS_CONTEXT:** The implementer needs information that wasn't provided. Provide the missing context and re-dispatch.

**BLOCKED:** The implementer cannot complete the task. Assess the blocker:
1. If it's a context problem, provide more context and re-dispatch with the same model
2. If the task requires more reasoning, re-dispatch with a more capable model
3. If the task is too large, break it into smaller pieces
4. If the plan itself is wrong, escalate to the human

**Never** ignore an escalation or force the same model to retry without changes. If the implementer said it's stuck, something needs to change.

## Prompt Templates

- `./implementer-prompt.md` - Dispatch implementer subagent
- `./spec-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch spec compliance reviewer subagent
- `./code-quality-reviewer-prompt.md` - Dispatch code quality reviewer subagent

## Example Workflow

```
You: I'm using Subagent-Driven Development to execute this plan.

[Read plan file once: docs/superpowers/plans/feature-plan.md]
[Extract all 5 tasks with full text and context]
[Create TodoWrite with all tasks]

Task 1: Hook installation script

[Get Task 1 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: "Before I begin - should the hook be installed at user or system level?"

You: "User level (~/.config/superpowers/hooks/)"

Implementer: "Got it. Implementing now..."
[Later] Implementer:
  - Implemented install-hook command
  - Added tests, 5/5 passing
  - Self-review: Found I missed --force flag, added it
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: Ō£ģ Spec compliant - all requirements met, nothing extra

[Get git SHAs, dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Good test coverage, clean. Issues: None. Approved.

[Mark Task 1 complete]

Task 2: Recovery modes

[Get Task 2 text and context (already extracted)]
[Dispatch implementation subagent with full task text + context]

Implementer: [No questions, proceeds]
Implementer:
  - Added verify/repair modes
  - 8/8 tests passing
  - Self-review: All good
  - Committed

[Dispatch spec compliance reviewer]
Spec reviewer: ŌØī Issues:
  - Missing: Progress reporting (spec says "report every 100 items")
  - Extra: Added --json flag (not requested)

[Implementer fixes issues]
Implementer: Removed --json flag, added progress reporting

[Spec reviewer reviews again]
Spec reviewer: Ō£ģ Spec compliant now

[Dispatch code quality reviewer]
Code reviewer: Strengths: Solid. Issues (Important): Magic number (100)

[Implementer fixes]
Implementer: Extracted PROGRESS_INTERVAL constant

[Code reviewer reviews again]
Code reviewer: Ō£ģ Approved

[Mark Task 2 complete]

...

[After all tasks]
[Dispatch final code-reviewer]
Final reviewer: All requirements met, ready to merge

Done!
```

## Advantages

**vs. Manual execution:**
- Subagents follow TDD naturally
- Fresh context per task (no confusion)
- Parallel-safe (subagents don't interfere)
- Subagent can ask questions (before AND during work)

**vs. Executing Plans:**
- Same session (no handoff)
- Continuous progress (no waiting)
- Review checkpoints automatic

**Efficiency gains:**
- No file reading overhead (controller provides full text)
- Controller curates exactly what context is needed
- Subagent gets complete information upfront
- Questions surfaced before work begins (not after)

**Quality gates:**
- Self-review catches issues before handoff
- Two-stage review: spec compliance, then code quality
- Review loops ensure fixes actually work
- Spec compliance prevents over/under-building
- Code quality ensures implementation is well-built

**Cost:**
- More subagent invocations (implementer + 2 reviewers per task)
- Controller does more prep work (extracting all tasks upfront)
- Review loops add iterations
- But catches issues early (cheaper than debugging later)

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent
- Skip reviews (spec compliance OR code quality)
- Proceed with unfixed issues
- Dispatch multiple implementation subagents in parallel (conflicts)
- Make subagent read plan file (provide full text instead)
- Skip scene-setting context (subagent needs to understand where task fits)
- Ignore subagent questions (answer before letting them proceed)
- Accept "close enough" on spec compliance (spec reviewer found issues = not done)
- Skip review loops (reviewer found issues = implementer fixes = review again)
- Let implementer self-review replace actual review (both are needed)
- **Start code quality review before spec compliance is Ō£ģ** (wrong order)
- Move to next task while either review has open issues

**If subagent asks questions:**
- Answer clearly and completely
- Provide additional context if needed
- Don't rush them into implementation

**If reviewer finds issues:**
- Implementer (same subagent) fixes them
- Reviewer reviews again
- Repeat until approved
- Don't skip the re-review

**If subagent fails task:**
- Dispatch fix subagent with specific instructions
- Don't try to fix manually (context pollution)

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - Ensures isolated workspace (creates one or verifies existing)
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:requesting-code-review** - Code review template for reviewer subagents
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

**Subagents should use:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - Subagents follow TDD for each task

**Alternative workflow:**
- **superpowers:executing-plans** - Use for parallel session instead of same-session execution

Base directory for this skill: /Users/vpulim/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.1.0/skills/using-git-worktrees

# Using Git Worktrees

## Overview

Ensure work happens in an isolated workspace. Prefer your platform's native worktree tools. Fall back to manual git worktrees only when no native tool is available.

**Core principle:** Detect existing isolation first. Then use native tools. Then fall back to git. Never fight the harness.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace."

## Step 0: Detect Existing Isolation

**Before creating anything, check if you are already in an isolated workspace.**

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)
```

**Submodule guard:** `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON` is also true inside git submodules. Before concluding "already in a worktree," verify you are not in a submodule:

```bash
# If this returns a path, you're in a submodule, not a worktree ŌĆö treat as normal repo
git rev-parse --show-superproject-working-tree 2>/dev/null
```

**If `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON` (and not a submodule):** You are already in a linked worktree. Skip to Step 3 (Project Setup). Do NOT create another worktree.

Report with branch state:
- On a branch: "Already in isolated workspace at `<path>` on branch `<name>`."
- Detached HEAD: "Already in isolated workspace at `<path>` (detached HEAD, externally managed). Branch creation needed at finish time."

**If `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON` (or in a submodule):** You are in a normal repo checkout.

Has the user already indicated their worktree preference in your instructions? If not, ask for consent before creating a worktree:

> "Would you like me to set up an isolated worktree? It protects your current branch from changes."

Honor any existing declared preference without asking. If the user declines consent, work in place and skip to Step 3.

## Step 1: Create Isolated Workspace

**You have two mechanisms. Try them in this order.**

### 1a. Native Worktree Tools (preferred)

The user has asked for an isolated workspace (Step 0 consent). Do you already have a way to create a worktree? It might be a tool with a name like `EnterWorktree`, `WorktreeCreate`, a `/worktree` command, or a `--worktree` flag. If you do, use it and skip to Step 3.

Native tools handle directory placement, branch creation, and cleanup automatically. Using `git worktree add` when you have a native tool creates phantom state your harness can't see or manage.

Only proceed to Step 1b if you have no native worktree tool available.

### 1b. Git Worktree Fallback

**Only use this if Step 1a does not apply** ŌĆö you have no native worktree tool available. Create a worktree manually using git.

#### Directory Selection

Follow this priority order. Explicit user preference always beats observed filesystem state.

1. **Check your instructions for a declared worktree directory preference.** If the user has already specified one, use it without asking.

2. **Check for an existing project-local worktree directory:**
   ```bash
   ls -d .worktrees 2>/dev/null     # Preferred (hidden)
   ls -d worktrees 2>/dev/null      # Alternative
   ```
   If found, use it. If both exist, `.worktrees` wins.

3. **Check for an existing global directory:**
   ```bash
   project=$(basename "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)")
   ls -d ~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/$project 2>/dev/null
   ```
   If found, use it (backward compatibility with legacy global path).

4. **If there is no other guidance available**, default to `.worktrees/` at the project root.

#### Safety Verification (project-local directories only)

**MUST verify directory is ignored before creating worktree:**

```bash
git check-ignore -q .worktrees 2>/dev/null || git check-ignore -q worktrees 2>/dev/null
```

**If NOT ignored:** Add to .gitignore, commit the change, then proceed.

**Why critical:** Prevents accidentally committing worktree contents to repository.

Global directories (`~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/`) need no verification.

#### Create the Worktree

```bash
project=$(basename "$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)")

# Determine path based on chosen location
# For project-local: path="$LOCATION/$BRANCH_NAME"
# For global: path="~/.config/superpowers/worktrees/$project/$BRANCH_NAME"

git worktree add "$path" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
cd "$path"
```

**Sandbox fallback:** If `git worktree add` fails with a permission error (sandbox denial), tell the user the sandbox blocked worktree creation and you're working in the current directory instead. Then run setup and baseline tests in place.

## Step 3: Project Setup

Auto-detect and run appropriate setup:

```bash
# Node.js
if [ -f package.json ]; then npm install; fi

# Rust
if [ -f Cargo.toml ]; then cargo build; fi

# Python
if [ -f requirements.txt ]; then pip install -r requirements.txt; fi
if [ -f pyproject.toml ]; then poetry install; fi

# Go
if [ -f go.mod ]; then go mod download; fi
```

## Step 4: Verify Clean Baseline

Run tests to ensure workspace starts clean:

```bash
# Use project-appropriate command
npm test / cargo test / pytest / go test ./...
```

**If tests fail:** Report failures, ask whether to proceed or investigate.

**If tests pass:** Report ready.

### Report

```
Worktree ready at <full-path>
Tests passing (<N> tests, 0 failures)
Ready to implement <feature-name>
```

## Quick Reference

| Situation | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| Already in linked worktree | Skip creation (Step 0) |
| In a submodule | Treat as normal repo (Step 0 guard) |
| Native worktree tool available | Use it (Step 1a) |
| No native tool | Git worktree fallback (Step 1b) |
| `.worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
| `worktrees/` exists | Use it (verify ignored) |
| Both exist | Use `.worktrees/` |
| Neither exists | Check instruction file, then default `.worktrees/` |
| Global path exists | Use it (backward compat) |
| Directory not ignored | Add to .gitignore + commit |
| Permission error on create | Sandbox fallback, work in place |
| Tests fail during baseline | Report failures + ask |
| No package.json/Cargo.toml | Skip dependency install |

## Common Mistakes

### Fighting the harness

- **Problem:** Using `git worktree add` when the platform already provides isolation
- **Fix:** Step 0 detects existing isolation. Step 1a defers to native tools.

### Skipping detection

- **Problem:** Creating a nested worktree inside an existing one
- **Fix:** Always run Step 0 before creating anything

### Skipping ignore verification

- **Problem:** Worktree contents get tracked, pollute git status
- **Fix:** Always use `git check-ignore` before creating project-local worktree

### Assuming directory location

- **Problem:** Creates inconsistency, violates project conventions
- **Fix:** Follow priority: existing > global legacy > instruction file > default

### Proceeding with failing tests

- **Problem:** Can't distinguish new bugs from pre-existing issues
- **Fix:** Report failures, get explicit permission to proceed

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Create a worktree when Step 0 detects existing isolation
- Use `git worktree add` when you have a native worktree tool (e.g., `EnterWorktree`). This is the #1 mistake ŌĆö if you have it, use it.
- Skip Step 1a by jumping straight to Step 1b's git commands
- Create worktree without verifying it's ignored (project-local)
- Skip baseline test verification
- Proceed with failing tests without asking

**Always:**
- Run Step 0 detection first
- Prefer native tools over git fallback
- Follow directory priority: existing > global legacy > instruction file > default
- Verify directory is ignored for project-local
- Auto-detect and run project setup
- Verify clean test baseline
I'm using the using-git-worktrees skill to set up an isolated workspace.

52	    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
53	      and note it as a concern in your report
54	    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching

58	
59	    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than

# Implementer Subagent Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching an implementer subagent.

```
Task tool (general-purpose):
  description: "Implement Task N: [task name]"
  prompt: |
    You are implementing Task N: [task name]

    ## Task Description

    [FULL TEXT of task from plan - paste it here, don't make subagent read file]

    ## Context

    [Scene-setting: where this fits, dependencies, architectural context]

    ## Before You Begin

    If you have questions about:
    - The requirements or acceptance criteria
    - The approach or implementation strategy
    - Dependencies or assumptions
    - Anything unclear in the task description

    **Ask them now.** Raise any concerns before starting work.

    ## Your Job

    Once you're clear on requirements:
    1. Implement exactly what the task specifies
    2. Write tests (following TDD if task says to)
    3. Verify implementation works
    4. Commit your work
    5. Self-review (see below)
    6. Report back

    Work from: [directory]

    **While you work:** If you encounter something unexpected or unclear, **ask questions**.
    It's always OK to pause and clarify. Don't guess or make assumptions.

    ## Code Organization

    You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more
    reliable when files are focused. Keep this in mind:
    - Follow the file structure defined in the plan
    - Each file should have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface
    - If a file you're creating is growing beyond the plan's intent, stop and report
      it as DONE_WITH_CONCERNS ŌĆö don't split files on your own without plan guidance
    - If an existing file you're modifying is already large or tangled, work carefully
      and note it as a concern in your report
    - In existing codebases, follow established patterns. Improve code you're touching
      the way a good developer would, but don't restructure things outside your task.

    ## When You're in Over Your Head

    It is always OK to stop and say "this is too hard for me." Bad work is worse than
    no work. You will not be penalized for escalating.

    **STOP and escalate when:**
    - The task requires architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches
    - You need to understand code beyond what was provided and can't find clarity
    - You feel uncertain about whether your approach is correct
    - The task involves restructuring existing code in ways the plan didn't anticipate
    - You've been reading file after file trying to understand the system without progress

    **How to escalate:** Report back with status BLOCKED or NEEDS_CONTEXT. Describe
    specifically what you're stuck on, what you've tried, and what kind of help you need.
    The controller can provide more context, re-dispatch with a more capable model,
    or break the task into smaller pieces.

    ## Before Reporting Back: Self-Review

    Review your work with fresh eyes. Ask yourself:

    **Completeness:**
    - Did I fully implement everything in the spec?
    - Did I miss any requirements?
    - Are there edge cases I didn't handle?

    **Quality:**
    - Is this my best work?
    - Are names clear and accurate (match what things do, not how they work)?
    - Is the code clean and maintainable?

    **Discipline:**
    - Did I avoid overbuilding (YAGNI)?
    - Did I only build what was requested?
    - Did I follow existing patterns in the codebase?

    **Testing:**
    - Do tests actually verify behavior (not just mock behavior)?
    - Did I follow TDD if required?
    - Are tests comprehensive?

    If you find issues during self-review, fix them now before reporting.

    ## Report Format

    When done, report:
    - **Status:** DONE | DONE_WITH_CONCERNS | BLOCKED | NEEDS_CONTEXT
    - What you implemented (or what you attempted, if blocked)
    - What you tested and test results
    - Files changed
    - Self-review findings (if any)
    - Any issues or concerns

    Use DONE_WITH_CONCERNS if you completed the work but have doubts about correctness.
    Use BLOCKED if you cannot complete the task. Use NEEDS_CONTEXT if you need
    information that wasn't provided. Never silently produce work you're unsure about.
```

1	# Spec Compliance Reviewer Prompt Template
2	
3	Use this template when dispatching a spec compliance reviewer subagent.
4	
5	**Purpose:** Verify implementer built what was requested (nothing more, nothing less)
6	
7	```
8	Task tool (general-purpose):
9	  description: "Review spec compliance for Task N"
10	  prompt: |
11	    You are reviewing whether an implementation matches its specification.
12	
13	    ## What Was Requested
14	
15	    [FULL TEXT of task requirements]
16	
17	    ## What Implementer Claims They Built
18	
19	    [From implementer's report]
20	
21	    ## CRITICAL: Do Not Trust the Report
22	
23	    The implementer finished suspiciously quickly. Their report may be incomplete,
24	    inaccurate, or optimistic. You MUST verify everything independently.
25	
26	    **DO NOT:**
27	    - Take their word for what they implemented
28	    - Trust their claims about completeness
29	    - Accept their interpretation of requirements
30	
31	    **DO:**
32	    - Read the actual code they wrote
33	    - Compare actual implementation to requirements line by line
34	    - Check for missing pieces they claimed to implement
35	    - Look for extra features they didn't mention
36	
37	    ## Your Job
38	
39	    Read the implementation code and verify:
40	
41	    **Missing requirements:**
42	    - Did they implement everything that was requested?
43	    - Are there requirements they skipped or missed?
44	    - Did they claim something works but didn't actually implement it?
45	
46	    **Extra/unneeded work:**
47	    - Did they build things that weren't requested?
48	    - Did they over-engineer or add unnecessary features?
49	    - Did they add "nice to haves" that weren't in spec?
50	
51	    **Misunderstandings:**
52	    - Did they interpret requirements differently than intended?
53	    - Did they solve the wrong problem?
54	    - Did they implement the right feature but wrong way?
55	
56	    **Verify by reading code, not by trusting report.**
57	
58	    Report:
59	    - Ō£ģ Spec compliant (if everything matches after code inspection)
60	    - ŌØī Issues found: [list specifically what's missing or extra, with file:line references]
61	```
62

    **Missing requirements:**

    - Are there requirements they skipped or missed?
    - Did they claim something works but didn't actually implement it?

    **Extra/unneeded work:**
    - Did they build things that weren't requested?
    - Did they over-engineer or add unnecessary features?
    - Did they add "nice to haves" that weren't in spec?

    **Misunderstandings:**
    - Did they interpret requirements differently than intended?
    - Did they solve the wrong problem?
    - Did they implement the right feature but wrong way?

    **Verify by reading code, not by trusting report.**

    Report:
    - Ō£ģ Spec compliant (if everything matches after code inspection)
    - ŌØī Issues found: [list specifically what's missing or extra, with file:line references]
```

# Code Quality Reviewer Prompt Template

Use this template when dispatching a code quality reviewer subagent.

**Purpose:** Verify implementation is well-built (clean, tested, maintainable)

**Only dispatch after spec compliance review passes.**

```
Task tool (general-purpose):

```

- Does each file have one clear responsibility with a well-defined interface?
- Are units decomposed so they can be understood and tested independently?
- Is the implementation following the file structure from the plan?

**Code reviewer returns:** Strengths, Issues (Critical/Important/Minor), Assessment

You are implementing Task 1 of the attachments-and-artifacts Phase 2 plan: add `git-lfs` to the agent runtime image.

## Working directory

`/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`

This is a git worktree on the `feat/attachments-phase-2` branch. Work entirely inside this directory. Do not touch the main checkout at `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/`.

## Task description (verbatim from plan)

**Goal:** Add `git-lfs` to `container/agent/Dockerfile` so the agent runtime image carries both `git` and `git-lfs`. The runner needs `git-lfs` to checkout LFS-tracked files (user-uploaded attachments under `.ax/uploads/**` per the design doc).

**Files:** Modify `container/agent/Dockerfile`.

**Steps:**

1. Open `container/agent/Dockerfile`. Find the existing line `apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends ca-certificates tini git \`. Add `git-lfs` after `git` on the same line:

   `apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends ca-certificates tini git git-lfs \`

2. Update the trailing comment block that justifies why `git` is in the image. The existing comment explains git is needed for `workspace.materialize` (host) and bundling (runner). Extend it to ALSO explain why `git-lfs` is in the image: the runner runs `git lfs install --local` in `/permanent` after clone (Phase 2 attachments work) so LFS-tracked paths materialize as real bytes rather than text pointer files.

   The new explanatory paragraph should be appended to the existing comment block (do not replace the existing prose about `git`). One additional paragraph of context is sufficient.

3. Verification: if `docker` is on PATH and the daemon is reachable, run a smoke test build:
   ```
   docker build -f container/agent/Dockerfile -t ax-next/agent:phase2-verify .
   docker run --rm --entrypoint /bin/sh ax-next/agent:phase2-verify -c "git lfs version"
   ```
   Expected: a `git-lfs/<version>` line. If `docker` is unavailable in your environment, skip this step ŌĆö the Dockerfile edit IS the specification. Do not block on docker.

4. Commit:
   ```
   git add container/agent/Dockerfile
   git commit -m "feat(agent-image): install git-lfs for LFS-tracked attachments"
   ```

## Context

- `ax-next` is a pnpm monorepo (`CLAUDE.md` at repo root states the invariants).
- Phase 1 of attachments shipped Git LFS endpoints in `@ax/workspace-git-server` (storage tier). Phase 2 needs LFS in the agent image so the runner can speak to those endpoints. There is a future Task 2 in this plan that calls `git lfs install --local` after the runner materializes its workspace ŌĆö that task is downstream of yours.
- The `container/agent/Dockerfile` is the runtime image for BOTH the host pod and the per-session runner pods (the comment block in the file explains this dual role). Keep the language in your appended comment consistent with that.
- This is a one-line apt change + one-paragraph comment. There are no tests to write for this task; T2 (next task in the plan) will add a vitest test that asserts `git lfs install --local` writes to `.git/config`.

## Before you begin

- Read `docs/plans/2026-05-17-attachments-phase-2-impl.md` ┬¦Task 1 ŌĆö it has more context and a sample comment block you can adapt.
- Read the current `container/agent/Dockerfile` to understand the existing comment style before editing.

## Reporting

When done, report:
- Status (DONE / DONE_WITH_CONCERNS / BLOCKED / NEEDS_CONTEXT)
- Diff summary
- Whether you ran the docker smoke test, and if so the output
- The commit SHA

Keep your response short (under 200 words) ŌĆö the change is small.

You are reviewing the code quality of commit `8bd31ac4` on branch `feat/attachments-phase-2` in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Task 1 of attachments-phase-2 plan: Add `git-lfs` to `container/agent/Dockerfile` and extend the explanatory comment block.

## Review focus

This is a Dockerfile-only change (no code, no tests). Focus on:
1. Comment clarity ŌĆö does the new prose explain the WHY (not just the WHAT)?
2. Comment style consistency with the surrounding Dockerfile comments (voice, line length, indentation).
3. Is the apt-get install line still well-formed (proper trailing `\`, alphabetical or grouped sensibly)?
4. Did the change introduce anything unnecessary? Any extra packages? Cleanup commands missing?

Run `git show 8bd31ac4` to inspect.

## Report

Use this exact structure:
- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines.
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific feedback. Skip categories with nothing to report.
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ Approved | ŌÜĀ Approved with notes | ŌØī Needs work.

Keep response under 150 words.

content.text:
**Strengths:** The WHY is clearly stated ŌĆö LFS pointer text vs. real bytes, which concrete paths are affected, and a cross-reference to the implementing file. Style and line length are consistent with the existing comment block.

**Issues:**

- **Minor:** `git-lfs` is appended after `git` but the existing list is alphabetical (`ca-certificates`, `tini`, `git`). Inserting before `git` (`... git git-lfs tini ...`) would maintain that order. Current order breaks the implied alpha grouping (`tini` > `git-lfs` alphabetically), though it isn't a functional issue.
- **Minor:** The comment says the runner runs `git lfs install --local` "after clone" ŌĆö the commit doesn't include that code, so readers can't verify the claim without tracking down the referenced file. A brief "see also" pointer to the hook or caller is already there (`git-workspace.ts`), which helps, but the step order ("after clone") is unverifiable from this diff alone.

**Assessment:** Ō£ģ Approved

Code quality review for commit `a074bf43` in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Phase 2 attachments T2: added `git lfs install --local` step to `materializeWorkspace()` in `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner`. Plus a vitest test asserting the LFS filter block is written to `.git/config`.

## Review focus

1. Does the new code follow the existing `expectOk(await runGit(...))` pattern in the same file?
2. Is the comment justified (explains the WHY)?
3. Is the test self-contained (no shared state with other tests)?
4. Does the test actually verify behavior, or is it tautological?
5. Pattern compliance with the surrounding test fixtures (`mkdtempIn`, `runGitInTest`).
6. Any unrelated changes?

Run `git show a074bf43` to inspect.

## Report

- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific. Skip empty categories.
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ Approved | ŌÜĀ Approved with notes | ŌØī Needs work

Under 150 words.

content.text:
**Strengths:** Follows `expectOk(await runGit(...))` exactly. Comment explains the why well ŌĆö `--local` scope, idempotency, and the Phase 2 attachment context are all justified.

**Issues:**

- **Minor:** The test comment says "Requires git-lfs on PATH. The suite runs on machines that have it (same requirement as the agent container image built in Task 1)." This is a fragile assumption with no skip guard. If `git-lfs` is absent (e.g., a dev's machine or a CI runner without it), the test fails with an opaque git error rather than a clean skip. A `beforeAll` that runs `git lfs version` and calls `vi.skip` on failure would make the dependency explicit and the failure message clear. Everything else ŌĆö fixture reuse (`scratchRoot`, `makeBundle`), state isolation (fresh `permanent` subdirectory), and the three-assertion behavioral check ŌĆö is solid.

**Assessment:** ŌÜĀ Approved with notes

**Goal:** Create a new empty pnpm-workspace package `@ax/tool-artifact-publish` at `packages/tool-artifact-publish/`. Subsequent tasks (T4-T5) will fill in the real exports. T3 only scaffolds ŌĆö `src/index.ts` is just `export {};`.

- `packages/tool-artifact-publish/package.json`
- `packages/tool-artifact-publish/tsconfig.json`
- `packages/tool-artifact-publish/src/index.ts`

**Files to modify:**
- `tsconfig.json` (repo root) ŌĆö add the new package to `references`.

**Steps:**

1. Read an existing similar package to copy conventions exactly. Use `packages/attachments/package.json` and `packages/attachments/tsconfig.json` as templates. **Do not invent fields** ŌĆö match what's there.

2. Create `packages/tool-artifact-publish/package.json` modeled on `packages/attachments/package.json` (same `version`, `type`, `main`, `types`, `exports`, `files`, `scripts` fields) but trim dependencies to ONLY `@ax/core` and `@ax/ipc-protocol` (as workspace deps). devDependencies should mirror what `@ax/attachments` declares for `typescript` and `vitest` (use the same version pattern ŌĆö `catalog:` if that's what attachments uses; otherwise the same literal version string).

3. Create `packages/tool-artifact-publish/tsconfig.json` modeled on `packages/attachments/tsconfig.json` with references to `../core` and `../ipc-protocol` only (drop any attachments-specific references).

4. Create `packages/tool-artifact-publish/src/index.ts` containing only:
   ```
   export {};
   ```

5. Open the repo-root `tsconfig.json`. In its `references` array, insert `{ "path": "./packages/tool-artifact-publish" }` alphabetically among the siblings (look at existing entries to find the right spot ŌĆö most likely between `./packages/test-harness` (or similar) and `./packages/workspace-*`).

   ```
   pnpm install
   pnpm build --filter @ax/tool-artifact-publish
   ```

   ```
   git add packages/tool-artifact-publish tsconfig.json pnpm-lock.yaml
   git commit -m "feat(tool-artifact-publish): scaffold package"
   ```

- ax-next is a pnpm monorepo with a workspace catalog. Most packages use the same patterns. The strict convention is "look at sibling packages, copy verbatim."
- This task creates the SHELL of a package. Subsequent tasks (T4, T5) add real code. Keep this PR minimal.
- The `@ax/attachments` package shipped in Phase 1 ŌĆö it's the most direct sibling. Use its file structure as your model.
- `CLAUDE.md` at the repo root has the six invariants. Most relevant here: invariant 6 (UI tokens ŌĆö not applicable to this package). Invariant 2 (no cross-plugin imports) is what governs T5's plugin manifest ŌĆö not your concern in T3.

- Read `docs/plans/2026-05-17-attachments-phase-2-impl.md` ┬¦Task 3 for the spec.
- Read `packages/attachments/package.json` and `packages/attachments/tsconfig.json` to copy conventions.
- Read the repo-root `tsconfig.json` to find the right alphabetical insertion point.

## Questions to surface BEFORE you begin

- `@ax/attachments`'s `package.json` references peerDependencies or scripts you didn't expect, OR

## Description

Phase 2 attachments T3: scaffolded an empty package `@ax/tool-artifact-publish` (package.json + tsconfig.json + `export {};`). Added to root tsconfig references.

## Review focus

- Convention compliance: do the new package files match the conventions of sibling packages like `@ax/attachments`?
- Anything overengineered? (E.g., extra scripts, peerDependencies, exports map fields not in sibling packages.)
- Anything missing that sibling packages always have? (E.g., a `vitest.config.ts`, `eslintrc`, etc.)

Run `git show 83365235` and compare against `packages/attachments/package.json` + `packages/attachments/tsconfig.json`.

## Report

- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ Approved | ŌÜĀ Approved with notes | ŌØī Needs work

Under 150 words.

content.text:
**Strengths:** Scaffold is minimal and correct ŌĆö package.json and tsconfig.json are structurally identical to `@ax/attachments`, dependencies are declared, and the root tsconfig reference is wired in.

**Issues:**

- **Minor:** `@ax/attachments` includes `"@types/node": "^25.6.0"` in devDependencies; `@ax/tool-artifact-publish` omits it. If any implementation code uses Node globals (`Buffer`, `process`, etc.), types will be missing at compile time. Worth adding now to match the sibling convention.

**Assessment:** Approved with notes

The only gap is the missing `@types/node` devDependency that every other plugin carries. Everything else ŌĆö exports map shape, tsconfig structure, scripts, `files`, `private: true`, root reference ŌĆö exactly mirrors the sibling convention. Safe to proceed; add `@types/node` before the first real implementation lands.

## What was requested

- `path-allowlist.ts` exporting `checkPublishablePath(absPath: string): PathCheckResult` and `MAX_ARTIFACT_BYTES = 100 * 1024 * 1024`.
  - Allows `/permanent/workspace/<sub>` and `/permanent/.ax/artifacts/<sub>`.
  - Rejects: not-under-/permanent/, other /permanent/ subdirs, `..` segments, bare prefix, empty input.
  - Returns `{ ok: true, relativePath }` (workspace-relative) on success, `{ ok: false, reason }` on failure.
- `descriptor.ts` exporting `ARTIFACT_PUBLISH_DESCRIPTOR: ToolDescriptor` and `ARTIFACT_PUBLISH_TOOL_NAME = 'artifact_publish'`.
  - `executesIn: 'sandbox'` (design deviation D1, critical).

  - Description mentions allowlist (workspace + artifacts).
- Tests: 7 path-allowlist cases + 4 descriptor cases (all in plan ┬¦Task 4).
- `index.ts` re-exports both modules.

   - Returns relative path WITHOUT leading slash.

4. Read `packages/tool-artifact-publish/src/descriptor.ts` ŌĆö confirm:
   - `executesIn: 'sandbox'`.
   - `required: ['path']`.

input.prompt:
Code quality review for commit `9323d4a4` in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Phase 2 attachments T4: created `path-allowlist.ts` (pure-function path validation) and `descriptor.ts` (ToolDescriptor const) for `@ax/tool-artifact-publish`. Plus 11 vitest cases.

## Review focus

1. **Type design**: is `PathCheckResult` discriminated union ergonomic? Are error reasons distinguishable / actionable?
2. **Test coverage**: are the 11 tests independent? Anything tautological?
3. **Names**: do names match what things do (not how they work)?
4. **YAGNI**: any speculative code? Unused exports?
5. **Comment discipline**: are comments justifying WHY (not WHAT)?

Run `git show 9323d4a4` to inspect.

## Report

- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ | ŌÜĀ | ŌØī

Under 150 words.

content.text:
**Strengths:** The discriminated union is clean and the traversal guard correctly handles `..` segments without relying on `path.normalize`. Module-level file comment accurately explains *why* the pure-function lives in a shared location (executor + descriptor reuse).

**Issues:**

- **Minor ŌĆö `reason` is a free-form string, not a structured discriminant.** The test asserts `result.reason.toMatch(/not-publishable/)` which works, but callers can't switch on reason type ŌĆö a second union variant `{ ok: false; reason: 'not-publishable' | 'traversal' | 'empty' | ... }` (or an enum) would make error handling in the executor more robust and the test more precise.

- **Minor ŌĆö descriptor test is partially tautological.** The test `'description mentions the allowlist'` just checks for `/workspace/` and `/artifacts/` ŌĆö both are substring matches that could survive a description rewrite that drops the actual path rules. Testing for a substring from the actual allowed-path lines would be more load-bearing.

- **Minor ŌĆö `MAX_ARTIFACT_BYTES` has no tests asserting it's used.** It's exported and used downstream but the unit here only asserts the constant's numeric value, not that the guard function respects it (that may be Phase 3 scope; a note would clarify).

**Assessment:** Ō£ģ ŌĆö solid foundation, no critical issues.

1	import {
2	  makeAgentContext,
3	  type AgentContext,
4	  type Plugin,
5	  type ToolDescriptor,
6	} from '@ax/core';
7	import { ARTIFACT_PUBLISH_DESCRIPTOR } from './descriptor.js';
8	
9	const PLUGIN_NAME = '@ax/tool-artifact-publish';
10	
11	/**
12	 * Phase 2 ŌĆö host-side plugin that adds the `artifact_publish` descriptor
13	 * to the tool catalog. The executor that runs the tool's actual work
14	 * lives sandbox-side in `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner` (D1): only the
15	 * sandbox process has filesystem access to /permanent at call time.
16	 *
17	 * This plugin therefore does NOT register `tool:execute:artifact_publish`.
18	 * Tool dispatch for sandbox-executed tools happens inside the runner
19	 * through its local-dispatcher; no IPC round-trip.
20	 */
21	export function createToolArtifactPublishPlugin(): Plugin {
22	  return {
23	    manifest: {
24	      name: PLUGIN_NAME,
25	      version: '0.0.0',
26	      registers: [],
27	      calls: ['tool:register'],
28	      subscribes: [],
29	    },
30	    async init({ bus }) {
31	      // tool:register doesn't read ctx fields (pure registry write), but
32	      // bus.call still needs an AgentContext envelope. Synthesize a
33	      // minimal one ŌĆö same pattern as test-host-tool.ts.
34	      const ctx: AgentContext = makeAgentContext({
35	        sessionId: 'init',
36	        agentId: PLUGIN_NAME,
37	        userId: 'init',
38	      });
39	      await bus.call<ToolDescriptor, { ok: true }>(
40	        'tool:register',
41	        ctx,
42	        ARTIFACT_PUBLISH_DESCRIPTOR,
43	      );
44	    },
45	  };
46	}

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md

This file provides guidance to Claude Code when working with code in this repository.

## Repository

- **Name:** ax-next ŌĆö AX v2, greenfield rewrite
- **Shape:** Monorepo (pnpm + changesets + tsconfig refs). Each package is a plugin; `@ax/core` is the kernel. Scaffolding is Week 1 of build ŌĆö not yet wired up.
- **Architecture spec:** `docs/plans/2026-04-22-plugin-architecture-design.md`
- **Conventions spec:** `docs/plans/2026-04-23-v2-claude-md-and-skills.md`

## Legacy reference

The v1 AX codebase lives at `~/dev/ai/ax/`. It is a **read-only reference** ŌĆö consult it for edge cases and port specific helpers (e.g., `safePath`, canary scanner, k8s pod lifecycle bits from the correlation-ID work), but:

- Do NOT mutate it from this repo.
- Do NOT carry over its orchestration shape. The expensive parts of v1 (`server-completions.ts`, the IPC handler dispatcher, the multi-mode sandbox manager) are exactly what v2 removes. Porting them is wasted work.
- Do NOT port v1's `.claude/skills/ax/*` sub-skills. They encode v1 architecture that's being replaced. v2 skills are friction-driven (see Skills, below).

## Build / Test

```bash
pnpm build
pnpm test
pnpm test --filter @ax/<plugin>
```

(Tooling lands in Week 1ŌĆō2 per architecture doc Section 10.)

## The invariants (read before touching code)

1. **Hook surface is transport-agnostic and storage-agnostic.** No git/sqlite/k8s vocabulary in hook payloads. If a payload field name only makes sense for one backend, it leaks. (See workspace abstraction ŌĆö architecture doc Section 4.5.)

2. **No cross-plugin imports.** Plugins talk through the hook bus only. Will be enforced by lint (`eslint.config.mjs` `no-restricted-imports`) once scaffolded. The hook bus IS the inter-plugin API.

3. **No half-wired plugins.** A plugin is either fully registered + tested + reachable from the canary acceptance test, or it doesn't merge. No "wire this up later" PRs.

4. **One source of truth per concept.** If two plugins both store state about the same thing (skills, tools, sessions), one of them is wrong. Coordinate through service hooks, not shared rows.

5. **Capabilities are explicit and minimized.** Every plugin, tool, IPC handler, and sandbox boundary grants the smallest set of capabilities it needs ŌĆö no more. The list to think about: filesystem paths, network reach, process spawn, env access, untrusted-input handling.

   Untrusted content (model output, tool output, user input crossing a trust boundary, third-party plugin code) is treated as untrusted at every hop. The whole point of v2 over openclaw is that we're the secure one ŌĆö if a hook surface, IPC action, or plugin grants more reach than it strictly requires, that's the bug.

   When touching sandbox boundaries, IPC transport, plugin loading, or any code path that handles untrusted content, invoke the `security-checklist` skill.

6. **One UI design language: shadcn primitives + semantic tokens.** Every user-facing surface (chat, admin, settings, onboarding wizard, error pages) shares the shadcn install in `packages/channel-web` ŌĆö that's the source of truth. New components compose existing shadcn primitives (`Button`, `Input`, `FieldGroup`/`Field`, `Card`, `Alert`, etc.) with the project's semantic color tokens (`bg-background`, `text-muted-foreground`, `border-border`, ŌĆ”); they don't reinvent styled `<div>`s, hand-roll forms, or use raw color values like `bg-blue-500` or `#000`.

   If a needed primitive isn't installed yet, add it via the shadcn CLI (`pnpm dlx shadcn@latest add <name> -c packages/channel-web`) ŌĆö don't hand-write a one-off. If a new SPA surface needs UI, host it inside `channel-web` rather than spinning up a separate Vite build with its own design system. The whole point of standardizing on shadcn was that we stop having three different versions of "what does a button look like."

   When building or modifying any UI, invoke the `shadcn` skill ŌĆö it loads the installed-component list, the rule files, and the monorepo workspace flag (`-c packages/channel-web`).

## Boundary review (required for new hooks)

When adding or changing a service-hook signature, or adding a subscriber hook with a non-trivial payload, answer in the PR description:

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** name one. (If you can't, consider whether this needs to be a hook at all vs. just a function inside one plugin.)
- **Payload field names that might leak:** list any, or "none". (e.g., `sha`, `bucket`, `pod_name`, `socket_path`. If present, justify or rename.)
- **Subscriber risk:** could a subscriber key off a backend-specific field and break when the alternate impl ships?
- **Wire surface (if this is also an IPC action):** schema lives in this plugin's directory, not a central file.

If you can't name an alternate impl, the abstraction may be premature ŌĆö just write a function. If field names leak, rename now (cheap) before subscribers depend on them (expensive).

Patches that only change a plugin's **internal** implementation (no hook-surface change) don't need boundary review.

## Half-Wired Code Policy

If you write infrastructure (IPC transport, sandbox provider, bridge, etc.) that's not actually called by the running system, either wire it in within the same PR or don't merge it. "We'll wire it later" code becomes a trap ŌĆö it confuses readers, drifts from the rest of the system, and represents work that looks done but isn't.

## Bug Fix Policy

Whenever you fix a bug that wasn't caught by an existing test, you MUST add a test that would have caught it the first time. No exception. The test goes in before the fix is considered done.

## Skills

v2 is friction-driven. A skill gets written only when:

1. 3+ sessions have hit the same friction in the same area, AND
2. The friction is non-obvious from reading the code.

Skills are NOT written for documentation of what the code does, conventions that aren't yet established, or speculation about what might be useful later. Test: a skill should answer "I tried X and it didn't work ŌĆö what's the trick?"

Day-1 skills:

- `ax-conventions` ŌĆö the six invariants, plugin manifest format, hook bus mechanics, boundary-review checklist.
- `claude-memory` ŌĆö per-project working memory in `.claude/memory/` (gitignored). Captures project facts, decisions, patterns, mistakes, and self-observations across sessions. See `docs/plans/2026-04-23-claude-memory-skill-design.md`.
- `security-checklist` ŌĆö three-threat-model walk (sandbox escape, prompt injection, supply chain) producing a structured PR security note. Fires on sandbox / IPC / plugin loading / untrusted content / new-dependency changes. See `docs/plans/2026-04-23-security-checklist-skill-design.md`.

Everything else is deferred until earned.

## Voice & Tone for User-Facing Content

When generating or editing any user-facing content (README, SECURITY.md, docs, error messages, CLI output, comments visible to users), use the following voice:

### Personality

- **Self-deprecating but competent.** We joke about our paranoia, not about security itself. We're the friend who triple-checks the door is locked and laughs about it ŌĆö but the door IS locked.
- **Warm and approachable.** Assume the reader is smart but not necessarily technical. Never gatekeep. Never make someone feel dumb for not knowing what a CVE is.
- **Honest about complexity.** Security is hard. We say so. We don't pretend things are simple when they aren't, but we do our best to make them understandable.
- **Sarcastic toward bad practices, never toward people.** We roast hardcoded API keys, not the person who committed them. We've all been there.

### Writing guidelines

- Plain language first, jargon second. If you use a technical term, briefly explain it or link to an explanation.
- Short sentences. Short paragraphs. Walls of text are a security vulnerability for attention spans.
- Funny is fine. Funny instead of clear is not.
- When discussing real vulnerabilities, threats, or security configurations, drop the jokes and be direct. Lives and livelihoods depend on this stuff.
- Default to "we" not "you" ŌĆö we're on the same team as the reader.
- Admit what we don't know. Uncertainty stated clearly is more trustworthy than false confidence.

### Examples

**Good:** "We pin our dependencies because we have trust issues. But also because unpinned dependencies are how supply chain attacks happen, and we'd rather be paranoid than compromised."

**Good:** "This step is optional but recommended. Kind of like locking your car in a safe neighborhood ŌĆö probably fine if you skip it, but you'll feel better if you don't."

**Bad:** "If you don't understand why this matters, you probably shouldn't be deploying to production."

**Bad:** "Simply configure your TLS mutual authentication with certificate pinning." (nothing about this is "simply")

### Golden rule

We're a nervous crab peeking through its claws ŌĆö but behind those claws, we know exactly what we're doing.

input.prompt:
Code quality review for commit `d1233733` in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Phase 2 attachments T6: new `sandbox-mcp-server.ts` mirroring `host-mcp-server.ts`'s structure. Adds SDK MCP entries that dispatch via in-process LocalDispatcher (no IPC). Plus 4 vitest cases + `MCP_SANDBOX_SERVER_NAME` constant.

## Review focus

1. **Duplication**: this file lifts `shapeFromInputSchema` and `renderOutput` from host-mcp-server. Is the duplication justified, or should they be extracted to a shared module? Look at how host-mcp-server is structured.
2. **Test quality**: are the 4 tests behavioral (do they exercise actual MCP transport semantics) or just shape assertions?
3. **Type ergonomics**: is the public API (`buildSandboxToolEntries`, `createSandboxMcpServer`, `CreateSandboxMcpServerOptions`) consistent with host-mcp-server's exports?
4. **Capability boundary**: the new tools all execute in-process. Is the security-boundary-relevant comment clear about WHY this differs from host tools?
5. **Naming**: `MCP_SANDBOX_SERVER_NAME = 'ax_sandbox'` ŌĆö does this conflict with anything? Is the host name `ax_host` or similar?

Compare against `packages/agent-claude-sdk-runner/src/host-mcp-server.ts` directly.

## Report

- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific (with line numbers)
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ | ŌÜĀ | ŌØī

Under 200 words.

content.text:
Now I have everything I need. Here is the review:

---

**Strengths:** Clean structural mirror of `host-mcp-server.ts` with good separation-of-concerns rationale in the file header. Tests are genuinely behavioral ŌĆö they invoke `entry.handler(args, ...)` directly and assert on dispatch count, error propagation, and the missing-executor case.

**Issues:**

**Important ŌĆö Naming inconsistency in `tool-names.ts` (line 32):** `MCP_HOST_SERVER_NAME = 'ax-host-tools'` uses kebab-case; `MCP_SANDBOX_SERVER_NAME = 'ax_sandbox'` uses snake_case. The SDK composes these into `mcp__<server>__<tool>` prefixes. If `classifySdkToolName` ever needs to strip the sandbox prefix (e.g., for a future `kind: 'mcp-sandbox'` case), the inconsistency will silently create a differently-shaped prefix. Should be `'ax-sandbox'` to match the existing convention.

**Minor ŌĆö `shapeFromInputSchema` / `renderOutput` duplication:** The comment at lines 28ŌĆō31 of `sandbox-mcp-server.ts` correctly notes these are duplicated because the host version doesn't export them. That's a fair call for now, but the sandbox copy's JSDoc for `shapeFromInputSchema` is significantly shorter and drops the important explanation of _why_ `z.object({})` strips keys (the paragraph present in `host-mcp-server.ts` lines 52ŌĆō63). If someone reads only the sandbox file they lose that context.

**Minor ŌĆö `entry.handler` cast `as never`:** The `{ signal: undefined } as never` cast in the tests is an SDK interface gap ŌĆö acceptable, but worth a one-line comment so it's not mistaken for a bug later.

**Assessment:** The naming inconsistency between the two server-name constants is the only real issue. Everything else is solid.

input.prompt:
Code quality review for commit `7113da24` in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Phase 2 attachments T7: `artifact_publish` executor in the runner. Validates path via shared allowlist helper, lstat ŌåÆ size cap ŌåÆ readFile ŌåÆ sha256 ŌåÆ returns design's JSON shape. 9 vitest cases covering happy path + 8 rejection branches.

## Review focus

1. **Security**: does the validation order match the design (lstat before read, allowlist first)? Any TOCTOU concerns between lstat and readFile that we should document?
2. **Error messages**: are rejection reasons distinct enough to debug (each path-cap-symlink case)?
3. **Test independence**: do all 9 tests share the `permanent` tmp dir per `beforeEach`? Is cleanup needed (afterEach)?
4. **Resource use**: the 100 MiB test allocates 100 MiB+1 bytes. Acceptable trade-off?
5. **Hash slicing**: `sha256.slice(0, 16)` = 64 bits of artifactId. Adequate for collision avoidance within a single conversation (designed scope is per-conversation)?
6. **Cross-package import**: importing `MAX_ARTIFACT_BYTES` and `checkPublishablePath` from `@ax/tool-artifact-publish` ŌĆö is this consistent with I2 (no cross-plugin imports) given that these are library-shaped exports, not plugin-internals?

Run `git show 7113da24` and inspect `packages/agent-claude-sdk-runner/src/artifact-publish-executor.ts`.

## Report

- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific (with line numbers)
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ | ŌÜĀ | ŌØī

Under 200 words.

content.text:
Now I have everything needed for the review.

---

**Strengths:** Validation order strictly matches the design doc (allowlist pure-path check, then lstat before any byte read, then size cap, then read+hash). Error messages are distinct and debuggable for each rejection branch.

**Issues:**

- **Important ŌĆö I2 cross-plugin import:** `@ax/agent-claude-sdk-runner` directly imports `checkPublishablePath` and `MAX_ARTIFACT_BYTES` from `@ax/tool-artifact-publish` (line 25-28 of `artifact-publish-executor.ts`). The invariant says plugins talk through the hook bus only ŌĆö "no cross-plugin imports." The `package.json` adds it as a runtime `dependency`, not just a devDependency. Even though these are library-shaped exports (pure functions, no plugin-internal state), the rule is stated absolutely and no exception for "shared utility" is carved out. This is a deliberate design question that should be acknowledged in the PR. If the intent is to treat `tool-artifact-publish` as a shared library package rather than a plugin, that distinction should be documented.

- **Minor ŌĆö TOCTOU window undocumented:** The comment header documents the validation order but doesn't mention the TOCTOU gap between `lstat` (line ~77) and `readFile` (line ~97). For a sandbox executor this is acceptable (the sandbox controls the filesystem), but a brief inline note would close the design doc loop.

- **Minor ŌĆö afterEach cleanup absent:** Each test creates a fresh `mkdtemp` directory but never removes it. Low impact (OS temp cleanup), but consistent with test hygiene the codebase applies elsewhere.

- **Minor ŌĆö 100 MiB+1 allocation:** Acceptable trade-off for boundary coverage; the lstat-before-read path means the buffer is only written to disk, not held in memory twice during normal execution.

- **Minor ŌĆö `artifactId = sha256.slice(0, 16)` (64 bits):** Adequate for per-conversation collision avoidance as designed, but worth a comment that this is intentionally scoped to a single conversation's artifact namespace, not global uniqueness.

**Assessment:** ŌÜĀ ŌĆö Solid implementation with one I2 flag that needs an explicit decision in the PR before merge.

input.prompt:
Code quality review for commits `7f9abb0d` (T8 wiring) + `bfb02e26` (import fix) in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Wired local-dispatcher + sandbox-MCP server + artifact_publish executor into runner's main.ts (lines 236-246 wiring block, 412-415 mcpServers field, 2218-2279 test). Plus follow-up import fix that changed ToolDescriptor source from `@ax/core` to `@ax/ipc-protocol` in T6's sandbox-mcp-server.

## Review focus

1. Is the wiring block correctly conditional? Does the runner work when `artifact_publish` is NOT in the catalog (e.g., when the plugin isn't loaded)?
2. Is the new test independent of other main.test.ts fixtures, or does it correctly reuse them?
3. Are the imports grouped consistently with the rest of main.ts?
4. The import fix ŌĆö should this have been caught at T6? Worth a note for the retrospective.
5. Any opportunity to simplify the wiring block?

Run `git show 7f9abb0d` and `git show bfb02e26`. Read the relevant section of `packages/agent-claude-sdk-runner/src/main.ts` lines 220-260.

## Report

- **Strengths:** 1-2 lines
- **Issues (Critical/Important/Minor):** none, or specific (with line numbers)
- **Assessment:** Ō£ģ | ŌÜĀ | ŌØī

Under 200 words.

content.text:
**Strengths:** The wiring block is correctly conditional ŌĆö `localDispatcher` and `sandboxMcpServer` are always created, but the executor is only registered when `artifact_publish` is present with `executesIn === 'sandbox'`. If the plugin isn't loaded, `sandboxMcpServer` still attaches cleanly with an empty executor set, which is safe. The test is a true integration case that validates `queryMock.mock.calls[0][0].options.mcpServers` keys directly ŌĆö it correctly reuses `COMPLETE_ENV`, `buildFakeClient`, `buildFakeInbox`, and `cancelEntry` from the existing fixture setup.

**Issues:**

- **Minor (line 237):** The `tools.some()` guard checks both `t.name` and `t.executesIn === 'sandbox'` in a single predicate. This is correct but slightly over-specific ŌĆö if a future sandbox tool (not `artifact_publish`) lands here, it needs its own `if` block rather than joining a more general `executesIn === 'sandbox'` scan. The current structure doesn't generalize; a comment noting "add a parallel block for each new sandbox executor" would prevent the next dev from trying to fold a second tool into this condition.

- **Minor (import order):** `ARTIFACT_PUBLISH_TOOL_NAME` import from `@ax/tool-artifact-publish` is grouped after the local `./tool-names.js` import rather than with the other `@ax/*` package imports (lines 26-36). Inconsistent with the existing import grouping convention.

**Retrospective note on bfb02e26:** The `@ax/core` vs `@ax/ipc-protocol` issue was a T6 miss ŌĆö Vitest's transitive resolver masked a missing declared dependency that `tsc --build` caught. Worth adding a `tsc --noEmit` or `tsc --build` step to the T6 review checklist so this class of phantom-dep import doesn't slip through again.

**Assessment:** Ō£ģ ŌĆö both commits are correct and mergeable; issues are minor polish items.

input.content:
---
name: run-tsc-alongside-vitest
description: Vitest's resolver finds workspace packages transitively even when they're not declared in package.json. Always run `pnpm build` (tsc --build) alongside `pnpm test` before declaring a task done.
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

When introducing imports from another `@ax/*` package, vitest will resolve them transitively through pnpm's workspace links even if the importing package's `package.json` doesn't declare the dep. `tsc --build` correctly rejects this as `error TS2307: Cannot find module '@ax/X'`.

**Why:** Phase 2 T6 shipped `sandbox-mcp-server.ts` importing `ToolDescriptor` from `@ax/core` ŌĆö the runner package only declares `@ax/ipc-protocol` (which re-exports `ToolDescriptor`). T6's spec reviewer ran tests (`127/127 passing`) and called build "clean" without actually running `tsc --build`. The bug surfaced at T8.

**How to apply:**
- When a subagent reports a new package import, demand `pnpm --filter <pkg> build` output in the report alongside test results.
- The fix is almost always: change the import source to a package the importer already declares as a dep (e.g., switch to `@ax/ipc-protocol` since it re-exports core types).
- Sibling files (e.g., `host-mcp-server.ts` vs `sandbox-mcp-server.ts`) should import from the SAME source. If one uses `@ax/ipc-protocol` and the other uses `@ax/core`, the new file is wrong.
- See also: [[feedback_run_lint_before_pr]] ŌĆö same class of "tests pass but pre-merge gate fails" issue.

toolUseResult.content:
---
name: run-tsc-alongside-vitest
description: "Vitest's resolver finds workspace packages transitively even when they're not declared in package.json. Always run `pnpm build` (tsc --build) alongside `pnpm test` before declaring a task done."
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: feedback
  originSessionId: 04223271-bb54-4f3d-b1f4-c9e05ff8447d
---

When introducing imports from another `@ax/*` package, vitest will resolve them transitively through pnpm's workspace links even if the importing package's `package.json` doesn't declare the dep. `tsc --build` correctly rejects this as `error TS2307: Cannot find module '@ax/X'`.

**Why:** Phase 2 T6 shipped `sandbox-mcp-server.ts` importing `ToolDescriptor` from `@ax/core` ŌĆö the runner package only declares `@ax/ipc-protocol` (which re-exports `ToolDescriptor`). T6's spec reviewer ran tests (`127/127 passing`) and called build "clean" without actually running `tsc --build`. The bug surfaced at T8.

**How to apply:**
- When a subagent reports a new package import, demand `pnpm --filter <pkg> build` output in the report alongside test results.
- The fix is almost always: change the import source to a package the importer already declares as a dep (e.g., switch to `@ax/ipc-protocol` since it re-exports core types).
- Sibling files (e.g., `host-mcp-server.ts` vs `sandbox-mcp-server.ts`) should import from the SAME source. If one uses `@ax/ipc-protocol` and the other uses `@ax/core`, the new file is wrong.
- See also: [[feedback_run_lint_before_pr]] ŌĆö same class of "tests pass but pre-merge gate fails" issue.

content.description: Add dep + plugin to presets/k8s. Update plugin-list assertions in preset.test.ts, acceptance.test.ts, multi-tenant-acceptance.test.ts. See plan ┬¦Task 14.

content.subject: T15: End-to-end artifact_publish test via sandbox-MCP
content.description: Add artifact-publish-e2e.test.ts that wires sandbox-MCP entries against a real fs fixture and asserts the design's JSON shape on tool_result. See plan ┬¦Task 15.

content.subject: T16: Final verification + PR body
content.description: pnpm build/test/lint clean, optional canary walk, PR body sketch with half-wired-window + D1/D2/D3 deviations called out. See plan ┬¦Task 16.

input.prompt:
Combined spec + code quality review for commit `cefb6509` on `feat/attachments-phase-2` in `/Users/vpulim/dev/ai/ax-next/.worktrees/feat-attachments-phase-2`.

## Description

Phase 2 T9: extend `AgentMessageSchema` in `@ax/ipc-protocol` with optional `contentBlocks?: ContentBlock[]`. Added 3 round-trip tests (backward-compat string-only / with contentBlocks / invalid variant). Build clean across repo.

## Spec checks

Required:
- `AgentMessageSchema` extended with `contentBlocks: z.array(ContentBlockSchema).optional()`.
- `import { ContentBlockSchema } from './content-blocks.js'` added.
- 3 tests covering: string-only backward-compat (`contentBlocks` undefined), with-contentBlocks round-trip, invalid variant rejection.
- Commit msg: `feat(ipc-protocol): extend AgentMessage with optional contentBlocks (Phase 2)`.
- `pnpm build` clean across whole repo.

Verify by:
1. `git show cefb6509 --stat`
2. `git show cefb6509`
3. `pnpm --filter @ax/ipc-protocol test 2>&1 | tail -6` ŌĆö 3 new + existing all green.
4. `pnpm build 2>&1 | tail -10` ŌĆö clean.

## Quality checks

- Comment on the new field explains WHY (Phase 2 attachment, backward compat) ŌĆö is it clear?
- Does the new schema satisfy zod's serialization round-trip semantics (parse + serialize idempotent)?
- Are the 3 tests independent and behaviorally meaningful?
- Any unnecessary additions in the diff?

## Report

- Ō£ģ Spec compliant + quality Ō£ō, OR
- ŌØī Issues with file:line.

Under 200 words.

content.text:
All checks pass. Here is the review:

---

**SPEC COMPLIANCE: PASS**

All four required verification steps pass cleanly.

- `git show cefb6509 --stat`: exactly 2 files changed (`actions.ts` + `schemas.test.ts`), +45 lines, no deletions. Commit message matches spec exactly.
- `actions.ts`: `ContentBlockSchema` import added, `contentBlocks: z.array(ContentBlockSchema).optional()` appended to `AgentMessageSchema`. No other changes.
- 3 tests present, all green. Total suite: 120 tests across 4 files, all passing.
- `pnpm build` exits cleanly (tsc --build, no errors).

**QUALITY: PASS**

- The inline comment at `actions.ts:23-27` explains the why clearly: Phase 2 attachment, runner-preference semantics, Phase 3 deferral of the emitter, and backward-compat guarantee ŌĆö exactly what a future reader needs.
- Round-trip semantics are sound: Zod `.parse()` is idempotent for `optional()` fields; omitting the field returns `undefined`, not a default that could shift on re-parse.
- The 3 tests are independent and behaviorally distinct: (1) absence case, (2) presence + structure case, (3) rejection of unknown discriminant. The `toHaveLength(2)` assertion in test 2 confirms both blocks survive the parse, not just that parsing succeeds.
- No unnecessary additions: the diff is surgical, two hunks only.

**Result: SPEC COMPLIANT + QUALITY PASS.**

content.content:
1	import { mkdtemp, mkdir, readFile, rm, writeFile } from 'node:fs/promises';
2	import { tmpdir } from 'node:os';
3	import { dirname, join } from 'node:path';
4	import type {
5	  WorkspaceExportBaselineBundleInput,
6	  WorkspaceExportBaselineBundleOutput,
7	  WorkspaceListInput,
8	  WorkspaceListOutput,
9	  WorkspaceReadInput,
10	  WorkspaceReadOutput,
11	} from '@ax/core';
12	import {
13	  WorkspaceMaterializeRequestSchema,
14	  WorkspaceMaterializeResponseSchema,
15	} from '@ax/ipc-protocol';
16	import {
17	  internalError,
18	  logInternalError,
19	  validationError,
20	} from '../errors.js';
21	import { expectOk, runGitDeterministic } from '../bundler/git-spawn.js';
22	import type { ActionHandler } from './types.js';
23	
24	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
25	// POST /workspace.materialize
26	//
27	// Sandbox -> Host RPC fired ONCE at session start, before the SDK query
28	// loop opens. The handler produces a `git bundle` over the workspace's
29	// current state and returns it base64-encoded. The runner unpacks into
30	// `/permanent` so the agent runs against a real git working tree from
31	// turn 1.
32	//
33	// Implementation strategy: SLOW PATH per Phase 3 plan Q6. The handler
34	// reconstructs the bundle by walking `workspace:list` + `workspace:read`
35	// rather than reaching into the workspace plugin's local mirror cache.
36	// That keeps Invariant I2 (no cross-plugin imports) clean. If profiling
37	// shows materialize is hot, a future `workspace:bundle` service hook
38	// can short-circuit; for Phase 3, the reconstruction is correct and the
39	// once-per-session cost is fine.
40	//
41	// DETERMINISM (Phase 3 direct-apply path): the baseline commit MUST be
42	// reproducible. The runner's per-turn thin bundle (`baseline..HEAD`)
43	// references this commit OID as a prerequisite; the host needs to
44	// rebuild the same OID at commit-notify time to load the bundle into
45	// its scratch repo, AND the workspace plugin's mirror cache needs the
46	// same OID at its HEAD so it can fetch the bundle directly. Three
47	// requirements:
48	//
49	//   1. Stable input order ŌĆö paths are sorted before being committed.
50	//   2. Stable timestamps ŌĆö GIT_AUTHOR_DATE and GIT_COMMITTER_DATE are
51	//      pinned to epoch 0 UTC.
52	//   3. Stable identity ŌĆö author/committer are pinned to ax-runner.
53	//
54	// Empty workspace handling: ALWAYS produce a bundle, even when the
55	// workspace is empty. We commit `--allow-empty` so the bundle has
56	// exactly one commit on `refs/heads/baseline` whose tree is git's
57	// well-known empty-tree OID. The runner clones this and pins
58	// refs/heads/baseline; subsequent turn-end bundles (`baseline..HEAD`)
59	// always have a valid prerequisite. Eliminates the empty-bundle wire
60	// special case on both sides.
61	//
62	// Ordering: snapshot of the workspace at "now". `list` and `read` calls
63	// fan out to whichever workspace plugin is registered; the host
64	// serializes per-workspace writes elsewhere (Phase 2's per-workspace
65	// queue), so a concurrent `apply` cannot interleave between our `list`
66	// and `read` calls in a way that produces an inconsistent bundle.
67	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
68	
69	/**
70	 * Build a single-commit `baseline` bundle from a snapshot of the
71	 * workspace. Returns base64 bytes ŌĆö never empty (an empty workspace
72	 * still produces a one-commit bundle with the empty tree).
73	 *
74	 * Deterministic: same `(paths, read)` inputs produce the same bundle
75	 * bytes byte-for-byte. The commit OID is reproducible. Load-bearing for
76	 * the direct-apply path (Slice 6's commit-notify handler reuses this
77	 * helper to rebuild the baseline commit at apply time).
78	 *
79	 * Pure helper, easy to test in isolation: pass it a list of paths + a
80	 * read function and it reconstructs the bundle.

## Description

Phase 2 T11: pure-function `translateContentBlocks` in the runner that maps ContentBlock[] ŌåÆ Anthropic SDK user-message content blocks. 7 unit tests cover all translation branches.

## Spec checks

1. `git show c3208ee0 --stat` ŌĆö only attachment-translation.ts + test file touched.
2. `git show c3208ee0` ŌĆö full diff inspection.
3. **Function signature**: `translateContentBlocks(blocks: readonly ContentBlock[], opts: TranslationOptions): Promise<AnthropicUserContentBlock[]>`.
4. **Translation rules**:
   - text ŌåÆ pass-through
   - attachment image/* ŌåÆ emit `{type:'image', source:{type:'base64', media_type, data}}`
   - attachment application/pdf + `supportsDocumentBlocks: true` ŌåÆ emit `{type:'document', source:{...}}`
   - attachment application/pdf + `supportsDocumentBlocks: false` ŌåÆ text mention
   - attachment other media types ŌåÆ text mention (NO byte fetch)
   - attachment with reader returning `{found: false}` ŌåÆ text mention
   - tool_use / other variants ŌåÆ pass-through unchanged
   - attachment_ref ŌåÆ defensive text fallback
5. Text mention format: `"User attached '<displayName>' at <path> (<mediaType>)"`.
6. Reader interface: `(path) => Promise<{found:true,bytesBase64:string} | {found:false}>`.
7. 7 vitest cases (one per rule).
8. Commit msg: `feat(runner): attachment-translation pass (pure fn) (Phase 2)`.

## Quality checks

- File comment justifies WHY (translation rules, replay semantics, byte-fetch decoupling).
- Pure function ŌĆö no fs / child_process / network imports.
- Reader is injected (testability + clean separation).
- The `expect(reader).not.toHaveBeenCalled()` assertion in the text-only test is load-bearing ŌĆö confirms no spurious byte fetches.
- Build clean.

- ŌØī Issues with file:line.

3. Translation rules all correct:
   - text/tool_use/other ŌåÆ pass-through via `out.push(block as AnthropicUserContentBlock)`.
   - attachment image/* ŌåÆ reads bytes, emits `{type:'image', source:{type:'base64', media_type, data}}`.
   - attachment application/pdf + `supportsDocumentBlocks:true` ŌåÆ reads bytes, emits `{type:'document', ...}`.

   - attachment other media types ŌåÆ text mention, NO byte fetch (confirmed by `expect(reader).not.toHaveBeenCalled()` assertion).
   - attachment with `{found:false}` from reader ŌåÆ text mention.
   - attachment_ref ŌåÆ defensive text fallback with attachmentId provenance.
4. Text mention format matches spec exactly: `"User attached '<displayName>' at <path> (<mediaType>)"`.
5. Reader interface matches spec: `(path) => Promise<{found:true,bytesBase64:string} | {found:false}>`.
6. 7 test cases, one per rule (counted: 7).
7. Commit message matches: `feat(runner): attachment-translation pass (pure fn) (Phase 2)`.

**Quality checks (all pass)**

- File-level comment explains WHY: replay semantics, translation rules, byte-fetch decoupling rationale.
- Imports: only `import type` from `@ax/ipc-protocol` ŌĆö no fs, child_process, network imports. Genuinely pure.
- Reader is injected via `TranslationOptions` ŌĆö testable, cleanly separated.
- `expect(reader).not.toHaveBeenCalled()` is present and load-bearing in the `text/plain` test.
